revision.text:
{{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Tsumi'''''|罪}} is a Japanese word that indicates the violation of legal, social or religious rules.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|last=Matsumoto|first=Shigeru|title=Tsumi|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%BD%AA/|work=[[Shogakukan]] Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|accessdate=16 August 2010}}</ref> It is most often used in the religious and moral sense.<ref name="yahoo"/> Originally, the word indicated a divine punishment due to the violation of a divine [[taboo]] through evil deeds, defilement (''[[kegare]]'') or disasters.<ref name=":0">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsumi"</ref> When translated in English as "sin", the term covers therefore only one of the three meanings of the Japanese word.<ref name="yahoo"/>
# Evil deeds<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # [[Kegare]]<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # Disasters<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" />
==History== The term evolved to its present form as a contraction of {{transl|ja|tsutsumu}} ({{lang|ja|障む・恙む}}, ''to hinder, be hindered, to have an accident, to have some trouble''),<ref>Definition from the Iwanami [[Kōjien]] Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsutsumu"</ref> a verb which very generally indicated the occurrence of a negative event.<ref name="yahoo"/> In ancient Japan the word thus indicated not only crimes and other forbidden human actions, but also diseases, disasters, pollution, ugliness and any other unpleasant object or fact.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[harae]]''.
==See also== * ''[[Harae]]'' * ''[[Kegare]]''
==References== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto and society]]
{{shinto-stub}}

# Evil deeds<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # [[Kegare]]<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # Disasters<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" />

Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[harae]]''.
==See also== * ''[[Harae]]'' * ''[[Kegare]]''

Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[harae]]''.
==See also== * ''[[Harae]]'' * ''[[Kegare]]''

Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[harae]]''.
==See also== * ''[[Harae]]'' * ''[[Kegare]]''

Japan|accessdate=16 August 2010}}</ref> It is most often used in the religious and moral sense.<ref name="yahoo"/> Originally, the word indicated a divine punishment due to the violation of a divine [[taboo]] through evil deeds, defilement (''[[kegare]]'') or disasters.<ref name=":0">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsumi"</ref> When translated in English as "sin", the term covers therefore only one of the three meanings of the Japanese word.<ref name="yahoo"/>
# Evil deeds<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # [[Kegare]]<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # Disasters<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" />
==History== The term evolved to its present form as a contraction of {{transl|ja|tsutsumu}} ({{lang|ja|障む・恙む}}, ''to hinder, be hindered, to have an accident, to have some trouble''),<ref>Definition from the Iwanami [[Kōjien]] Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsutsumu"</ref> a verb which very generally indicated the occurrence of a negative event.<ref name="yahoo"/> In ancient Japan the word thus indicated not only crimes and other forbidden human actions, but also diseases, disasters, pollution, ugliness and any other unpleasant object or fact.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[harae]]''.
==See also== * ''[[Harae]]'' * ''[[Kegare]]''
==References== {{reflist}}
[[category:Shinto]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsumi]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tsumi]][[category:Shinto and society]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Tsumi]]‎]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tsumi]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] {{shinto-stub}}

The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first Category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[harae]]''.

The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first Category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[harae]]''.
==See also== * ''[[Harae]]'' * ''[[Kegare]]''

{{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Tsumi'''''|罪}} is a Japanese word that indicates the violation of legal, social or religious rules.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|last=Matsumoto|first=Shigeru|title=Tsumi|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%BD%AA/|work=[[Shogakukan]] Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|accessdate=16 August 2010}}</ref> It is most often used in the religious and moral sense.<ref name="yahoo"/> Originally, the word indicated a divine punishment due to the violation of a divine [[taboo]] through evil deeds, defilement (''[[kegare]]'') or disasters.<ref name=":0">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsumi"</ref> When translated in English as "sin", the term covers therefore only one of the three meanings of the Japanese word.<ref name="yahoo"/>
# Evil deeds<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # [[Kegare]]<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # Disasters<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" />
==History== The term evolved to its present form as a contraction of {{transl|ja|tsutsumu}} ({{lang|ja|障む・恙む}}, ''to hinder, be hindered, to have an accident, to have some trouble''),<ref>Definition from the Iwanami [[Kōjien]] Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsutsumu"</ref> a verb which very generally indicated the occurrence of a negative event.<ref name="yahoo"/> In ancient Japan the word thus indicated not only crimes and other forbidden human actions, but also diseases, disasters, pollution, ugliness and any other unpleasant object or fact.<ref name="yahoo"/>
The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first Category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[harae]]''.
==See also== * ''[[Harae]]'' * ''[[Kegare]]''
==References== {{reflist}}
‎]]
{{shinto-stub}}

==History== The term evolved to its present form as a contraction of {{transl|ja|tsutsumu}} ({{lang|ja|障む・恙む}}, ''to hinder, be hindered, to have an accident, to have some trouble''),<ref>Definition from the Iwanami [[Kōjien]] Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsutsumu"</ref> a verb which very generally indicated the occurrence of a negative event.<ref name="yahoo"/> In ancient Japan the word thus indicated not only crimes and other forbidden human actions, but also diseases, disasters, pollution, ugliness and any other unpleasant object or fact.<ref name="yahoo"/> The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first Category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[harae]]''.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tsumi]][[es:Tsumi]][[fr:Tsumi]][[it:Tsumi]][[ja:天つ罪・国つ罪]][[pl:Tsumi]][[ru:Цуми]][[zh:罪 (日本詞語)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3541665]] Wikidata name: tsumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Tsumi'''''|罪}} is a Japanese word that indicates the violation of legal, social or religious rules.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|last=Matsumoto|first=Shigeru|title=Tsumi|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%BD%AA/|work=[[Shogakukan]] Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|accessdate=16 August 2010}}</ref> It is most often used in the religious and moral sense.<ref name="yahoo"/> Originally, the word indicated a divine punishment due to the violation of a divine [[taboo]] through evil deeds, defilement (''[[kegare]]'') or disasters.<ref name=":0">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsumi"</ref> When translated in English as "sin", the term covers therefore only one of the three meanings of the Japanese word.<ref name="yahoo"/>
# Evil deeds<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # [[Kegare]]<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # Disasters<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" />
==History== The term evolved to its present form as a contraction of {{transl|ja|tsutsumu}} ({{lang|ja|障む・恙む}}, ''to hinder, be hindered, to have an accident, to have some trouble''),<ref>Definition from the Iwanami [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]] Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsutsumu"</ref> a verb which very generally indicated the occurrence of a negative event.<ref name="yahoo"/> In ancient Japan the word thus indicated not only crimes and other forbidden human actions, but also diseases, disasters, pollution, ugliness and any other unpleasant object or fact.<ref name="yahoo"/> The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first Category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[:en:Harae|Harae]]''.
==See also== * ''[[:en:Harae|Harae]]'' * ''[[Kegare]]''
==References== {{reflist}}
‎]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tsumi]][[es:Tsumi]][[fr:Tsumi]][[it:Tsumi]][[ja:天つ罪・国つ罪]][[pl:Tsumi]][[ru:Цуми]][[zh:罪 (日本詞語)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tsumi]][[es:Tsumi]][[fr:Tsumi]][[it:Tsumi]][[ja:天つ罪・国つ罪]][[pl:Tsumi]][[ru:Цуми]][[zh:罪 (日本詞語)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3541665]] Wikidata name: tsumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Tsumi'''''|罪}} is a Japanese word that indicates the violation of legal, social or religious rules.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|last=Matsumoto|first=Shigeru|title=Tsumi|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%BD%AA/|work=[[Shogakukan]] Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|accessdate=16 August 2010}}</ref> It is most often used in the religious and moral sense.<ref name="yahoo"/> Originally, the word indicated a divine punishment due to the violation of a divine [[taboo]] through evil deeds, defilement (''[[kegare]]'') or disasters.<ref name=":0">Iwanami {{nihongo|[[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]]|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsumi"</ref> When translated in English as "sin", the term covers therefore only one of the three meanings of the Japanese word.<ref name="yahoo"/>
# Evil deeds<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # [[Kegare]]<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # Disasters<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" />
==History== The term evolved to its present form as a contraction of {{transl|ja|tsutsumu}} ({{lang|ja|障む・恙む}}, ''to hinder, be hindered, to have an accident, to have some trouble''),<ref>Definition from the Iwanami [[:en:Kōjien|Kōjien]] Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsutsumu"</ref> a verb which very generally indicated the occurrence of a negative event.<ref name="yahoo"/> In ancient Japan the word thus indicated not only crimes and other forbidden human actions, but also diseases, disasters, pollution, ugliness and any other unpleasant object or fact.<ref name="yahoo"/> The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first Category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''[[:en:Harae|Harae]]''.
==See also== * ''[[:en:Harae|Harae]]'' * ''[[Kegare]]''
==References== {{reflist}}
‎]]

{{wikidata link|Q3541665}} [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto and society]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tsumi]][[es:Tsumi]][[fr:Tsumi]][[it:Tsumi]][[ja:天つ罪・国つ罪]][[pl:Tsumi]][[ru:Цуми]][[zh:罪 (日本詞語)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tsumi]][[es:Tsumi]][[fr:Tsumi]][[it:Tsumi]][[ja:天つ罪・国つ罪]][[pl:Tsumi]][[ru:Цуми]][[zh:罪 (日本詞語)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tsumi]][[es:Tsumi]][[fr:Tsumi]][[it:Tsumi]][[ja:天つ罪・国つ罪]][[pl:Tsumi]][[ru:Цуми]][[zh:罪 (日本詞語)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3541665]] Wikidata name: tsumi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Tsumi'''''|罪}} is a Japanese word that indicates the violation of legal, social or religious rules.<ref name="yahoo">{{cite web|last=Matsumoto|first=Shigeru|title=Tsumi|url=http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%BD%AA/|work=[[Shogakukan]] Encyclopedia|publisher=Yahoo! Japan|accessdate=16 August 2010}}</ref> It is most often used in the religious and moral sense.<ref name="yahoo"/> Originally, the word indicated a divine punishment due to the violation of a divine [[taboo]] through evil deeds, defilement (''[[kegare]]'') or disasters.<ref name=":0">Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsumi"</ref> When translated in English as "sin", the term covers therefore only one of the three meanings of the Japanese word.<ref name="yahoo"/>
# Evil deeds<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # [[Kegare]]<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # Disasters<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" />
==History== The term evolved to its present form as a contraction of {{transl|ja|tsutsumu}} ({{lang|ja|障む・恙む}}, ''to hinder, be hindered, to have an accident, to have some trouble''),<ref>Definition from the Iwanami {{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsutsumu"</ref> a verb which very generally indicated the occurrence of a negative event.<ref name="yahoo"/> In ancient Japan the word thus indicated not only crimes and other forbidden human actions, but also diseases, disasters, pollution, ugliness and any other unpleasant object or fact.<ref name="yahoo"/> The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first Category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=Harae}}''.
==See also== * ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=Harae}}'' * ''[[Kegare]]''
==References== {{reflist}}
‎]]
{{shinto-stub}}

Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=Harae}}''.

# Evil deeds<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # [[Kegare]]<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" /> # Disasters<ref name=":0" /><ref name="yahoo" />
==History== The term evolved to its present form as a contraction of {{transl|ja|tsutsumu}} ({{lang|ja|障む・恙む}}, ''to hinder, be hindered, to have an accident, to have some trouble''),<ref>Definition from the Iwanami {{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|WD=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version, "Tsutsumu"</ref> a verb which very generally indicated the occurrence of a negative event.<ref name="yahoo"/> In ancient Japan the word thus indicated not only crimes and other forbidden human actions, but also diseases, disasters, pollution, ugliness and any other unpleasant object or fact.<ref name="yahoo"/> The [[Engishiki]], a 927 AD Japanese book of laws and regulations, for example, distinguishes two kinds of ''tsumi'', the {{nihongo|Amatsutsumi|天津罪|heaven tsumi}} and the {{nihongo|kunitsutsumi|国津罪|land tsumi|}}.<ref name="yahoo"/> The first Category deals with infractions against property, the second mainly with infractions against people. Some of the ''tsumi'' have to do with disease and natural disasters, and are not therefore ''sins'' in the modern sense, but order perturbations (''[[kegare]]'') which had to be dealt with and solved by the person or persons concerned in certain ways, for example through purification rites called ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=Harae|1=Harae}}''.
==See also== * ''{{ill|Harae|en|Harae|WD=Q3127244|lt=Harae|1=Harae}}'' * ''[[Kegare]]''
==References== {{reflist}}
‎]]
